title: St. Anthony of the Caves of Kiev & St. Agatho Pope of Rome
narrative:
On the tenth day of Hamle, the Ethiopian Church honours Saint Anthony of the Caves of Kiev, the father of monasticism in Kievan Rus and one of the most significant figures in the transmission of the hesychast tradition from Mount Athos to the Slavic world. Anthony was a Ukrainian monk who made two pilgrimages to Mount Athos, where he absorbed the desert tradition of the Egyptian and Palestinian fathers as it had been preserved and developed in the Athonite communities, and who returned to Kiev to establish the Monastery of the Caves — the Pecherska Lavra — which became the mother church of Russian monasticism. The caves in which Anthony and his first disciples dwelt are still venerated as sacred space, and the monastery he founded has never ceased to be a centre of prayer and theological formation.

Saint Agatho, Pope of Rome in the late seventh century, is commemorated primarily for his role in the Third Council of Constantinople, which condemned Monothelitism — the teaching that Christ had only one will — and affirmed the full duality of wills in Christ, human and divine, in accordance with the Chalcedonian definition. Agatho prepared the council with extraordinary theological care, sending a letter that Gregory the Great would have recognized as worthy of himself, and dying before the council's conclusions were formally promulgated. His contribution to the precise articulation of Christology, coming a generation after Maximus the Confessor had suffered exile and mutilation for the same truth, is one of the great acts of papal theological leadership.

Anthony of Kiev and Agatho of Rome represent the two modes in which the dogmatic tradition is preserved: the monastic and the episcopal. Agatho preserved it through conciliar definition and doctrinal authority; Anthony preserved it through the daily practice of prayer, fasting, and communal life in accordance with the patristic tradition. Neither can substitute for the other. A Christianity that has councils without monasteries has defined the faith but lost the means of living it; a Christianity that has monasteries without councils has the life but lacks the protection of precise articulation. May their intercession sustain the Church in both modes of fidelity. Amen.
